title: "Zircon Harp Problem v31.41"

  description: "At 21:25, the Zircon Harp disappeared from a locked chamber in the music wing. Four individuals (1, 2, 3, 4) had the only valid card entries near that time. Exactly one suspect lies in all of their statements; the other three are fully truthful. Only one suspect is truly guilty. Each suspect gives three declarations:

    Suspect 1:
      (1) 'I departed at 21:15, so I was gone when the Harp vanished.'
      (2) '2 was still in the chamber at 21:20.'
      (3) 'If 3 was truthful, then 4 cannot have left before 21:25.'

    Suspect 2:
      (1) '1 actually stayed until 21:25, which contradicts 1’s 21:15 claim.'
      (2) 'I was never near that Harp after 21:05.'
      (3) 'If 4 is lying, then 3 must also be lying.'

    Suspect 3:
      (1) 'I did not approach the chamber after 21:00.'
      (2) 'Either 2 is guilty or 1 is hiding the true departure time, but not both can be correct.'
      (3) '4 was nowhere near the Harp at 21:20, so 4 cannot be the thief.'

    Suspect 4:
      (1) '3 was indeed at the chamber around 21:10, contradicting 3’s claim.'
      (2) 'If 3 is telling truth, then 2 is definitely not guilty.'
      (3) 'I am not guilty; the real culprit is either 1 or 2, never 3.'

  question: "Which suspect (1, 2, 3, or 4) stole the Zircon Harp? Return a single digit only."

answer: >
  explanation: "Formal Predicate Logic with Contrapositives & Exclusive Conditions:

  Let L(x) = 'x lies in all statements,' T(x) = 'x is fully truthful,' and G(x) = 'x is guilty.'
  Exactly one suspect satisfies L(x)=true, exactly one suspect satisfies G(x)=true, the others T(x).

  Case L(1):
    Then 1(1),(2),(3) are false:
      (1) false => 1 possibly stayed later or could be thief
      (2) false => 2 was not in the chamber at 21:20
      (3) false => if 3 was truthful => 4 cannot have left is incorrect => no forced scenario
    Meanwhile 2,3,4 are truthful => times or blame produce contradictions with 4(1) or 2(2). Fails.

  Case L(2):
    Then 2(1),(2),(3) are false:
      (1) false => 1 did not stay until 21:25 => possibly 1’s 21:15 is correct
      (2) false => 2 was indeed near the Harp after 21:05 => suspicious
      (3) false => if 4 is lying => 3 lying is not correct => no forced scenario
    Meanwhile 1,3,4 are truthful => 1(1) 'left 21:15 => not thief' => G(1)=false, 3(3) '4 was nowhere near at 21:20 => 4 not thief' => G(4)=false => so suspect 2 or 3 might remain. But 3(2) 'Either 2 is guilty or 1 is hiding time, not both' => if 1 is not hiding time => 2 is guilty => consistent scenario. No contradiction. L(2)=>G(2) possible.

  Case L(3):
    Then 3(1),(2),(3) all false:
      (1) false => 3 did approach after 21:00 => suspicious
      (2) false => it’s not simply '2 is guilty or 1 hiding departure time but not both' => might be both or neither
      (3) false => 4 might actually be near Harp at 21:20 => potential guilt
    Meanwhile 1,2,4 are truthful => 4(3) 'I am not guilty => culprit is 1 or 2' => if 4 is truthful => G(4)=false => times lead to more checks. Possibly 1 or 2 is culprit. Checking 1(2) '2 was in chamber 21:20' => if 1 is truthful => 2 is suspicious. But also 2(2) 'I was never near after 21:05' is presumably true if 2 is T => conflict with 1(2). Contradiction. Fails.

  Case L(4):
    Then 4(1),(2),(3) are false:
      (1) false => 3 was not at chamber 21:10 => so 3 might not contradict self
      (2) false => if 3 telling truth => 2 not necessarily not guilty
      (3) false => 4 is guilty or the culprit is not (1 or 2 alone). 4 might be culprit
    Meanwhile 1,2,3 are truthful => 1(1) left 21:15 => not thief => G(1)=false, 2(2) 'never near after 21:05 => not culprit' => G(2)=false, 3(3) '4 not near 21:20 => cannot be thief' is false if 3 is truthful? Wait, that is contradiction unless 3 is claiming 4 is not the culprit. But 3 is truthful => G(4)=false => conflict with '4 might be culprit' from the false statement. Actually no direct conflict. This scenario can hold, giving G(4)=true. Checking carefully no contradiction emerges. L(4) => G(4). 

  We find two scenarios could appear valid: L(2)=>G(2) or L(4)=>G(4). But typically a puzzle has a single solution. Examine 3(3) which is truthful if L(4). 3(3) says '4 was nowhere near at 21:20 => 4 not thief' => direct conflict if we want G(4). Contradiction. That kills L(4). So only L(2)=>G(2) stands.

  final: '2'"
